food_name: Com Ga Xoi Mo (Vietnamese Crispy Fried Chicken Rice)
aliases: Vietnamese poached and fried chicken rice

english: Vietnamese poached and fried chicken rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Com Ga Xoi Mo. The broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002700) is used as the closest defensible parent class. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the primary protein component anchor for this composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Com Ga Xoi Mo. A0FJP (cooked chicken) is used as the dominant protein anchor. The rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this Vietnamese composite dish. SNOMED CT food coding is not applicable at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Com Ga Xoi Mo weighs approximately 550 g, comprising roughly 200–220 g of deep-fried chicken pieces, 250–270 g of steamed jasmine rice, and 50–60 g of fresh vegetables and dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia — composite dish estimation using chicken (fried) and steamed white rice components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single FAO/INFOODS entry exists for Com Ga Xoi Mo as a composite dish. Values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for deep-fried chicken (~40% by weight) and steamed white rice (~50% by weight), with minor contributions from vegetables and fish sauce dipping sauce (~10%). The input record values (energy 172.73 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 10.91 g per 100 g) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken (NDB 05062) and cooked white rice (NDB 20045). Sodium 327 mg/100 g reflects fish sauce contribution and is plausible for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Com Ga Xoi Mo as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The chicken and fat components do not contribute meaningfully to GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 60.0) / 100 = 43.2, where available carbohydrate per 550 g serving ≈ 10.91 g/100 g × 550 g / 100 = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dipping sauce (327 mg/100 g; ~1800 mg per full 550 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request the fish sauce dipping sauce on the side or substitute with a low-sodium alternative, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake below 600 mg per meal where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI 72) combined with deep-fried chicken fat worsens postprandial glucose and insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half, and request grilled or steamed chicken instead of deep-fried to lower the combined glycemic and fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), and sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden; if consumed, portion should not exceed 200–250 g and grilled chicken should be substituted for the deep-fried preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request grilled or steamed chicken, and monitor for joint flare responses to nightshade vegetables present in the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm gà xối mỡ (Com Ga Xoi Mo) is a popular Vietnamese dish consisting of crispy deep-fried chicken served alongside fragrant steamed jasmine rice, fresh sliced cucumber and tomato, and a fish sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm). The dish is distinguished by its preparation technique: the chicken is first poached or simmered until cooked through, then submerged in very hot oil to achieve an intensely crispy, golden-brown skin while retaining moist, tender meat inside. The rice is typically cooked with chicken stock and aromatics such as garlic and shallots, giving it a savoury, fragrant character. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 173 kcal, 10 g protein, 10 g fat (2.7 g saturated), and 10.9 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 327 mg reflecting the fish sauce component. The glycemic index is high (approximately 72) driven by the white jasmine rice, making the dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease without modification. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and of concern for rheumatoid arthritis patients. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 550 g. The dish is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants and food courts across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, and is considered a comfort meal suitable for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for deep-fried chicken and steamed white rice, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single approved-source entry exists for Com Ga Xoi Mo as a composite dish. The input values from the GrabFood record are consistent with the component-weighted estimate and have been retained. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact dish-level composition depends on restaurant-specific preparation ratios.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA table; estimated potassium ~230 mg/100 g was noted in the input record but is not confirmed by an approved source and has therefore been set to -1 pending verification. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the composite level as of 2024. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific certification; the dish may be halal-certifiable if prepared with halal-certified fish sauce and chicken. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, but the dish is of Vietnamese origin; this has been corrected to Vietnam. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database.
